title: Unified Framework for Quantum Resource Recycling via Instrument-Dependent Back-action
authors: Zinuo Cai, Jianxin Song, Changliang Ren
abstract: Accurate characterization of measurement backaction is crucial for understanding the limits of reusing quantum correlations in sequential scenarios. Here, we develop a unified quantum-instrument framework that goes beyond simple measurement statistics, explicitly attributing correlation sharing to Kraus-structure-dependent backaction. By tracing operational differences to this underlying physical mechanism, our framework integrates previously disparate strategies. Within this formulation, we derive general conditions for unbounded unilateral nonlocality sharing across arbitrarily many observers. The framework further reveals that Bell nonlocality remains shareable in bilateral sequential scenarios. These results establish quantum instruments, rather than POVMs alone, as the fundamental constraints on correlation sharing, providing a unified conceptual framework for quantum resource recycling.
summary: This paper introduces a unified framework to explain how the internal structure of quantum measurements determines the ability to reuse quantum correlations. It proves that Bell nonlocality can be shared among an unlimited number of sequential observers by tailoring measurement backaction. The study demonstrates that quantum instruments, not just measurement statistics, are the fundamental constraints on quantum resource recycling.
main_problem: Understanding the fundamental limits of recycling quantum correlations, such as Bell nonlocality, in sequential multi-observer measurement scenarios.
main_result: The authors establish a unified framework showing that correlation sharing is governed by quantum-instrument-dependent backaction rather than just POVM statistics, proving that both unilateral and bilateral Bell nonlocality can be shared across arbitrary numbers of observers.
method: The study uses a Kraus-decomposition-based functional framework to analyze measurement backaction, employing analytical derivations, small-angle limit analysis, and MultiStart Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) for optimization.
model_or_system: The framework considers a single-qubit binary measurement observable and a pure entangled two-qubit state, evaluating two measurement reduction models: the Elliptical Reduction Model (based on Lüders decomposition) and the Linear Reduction Model.
key_observables: CHSH violation values (S-parameters), Bell nonlocality, and coherence retention (reduction factor F).
important_parameters: Measurement sharpness (alpha), outcome bias (beta), measurement angles (phi, theta), and the number of sequential observers (N).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes specific measurement ansatzes, such as the elliptical and linear reduction models, and relies on the small-angle limit (phi to 0) for asymptotic scaling proofs.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates how the elliptical reduction model constrains coherence reduction to an elliptical trajectory; Figure 2 shows the Pareto frontier of CHSH violations in the bilateral scenario.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of correlation recycling, develops a unified quantum-instrument framework, analyzes unilateral sharing limits, explores bilateral sharing scenarios via optimization, and provides asymptotic scaling proofs for infinite sharing.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a fundamental physical mechanism (instrument-dependent backaction) for the degradation of quantum resources during sequential measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Instrument-dependent measurement backaction. (a) Ellipti- cal reduction model. Coherent superposition of measurement chan- nels (L¨uders decomposition) constrains the transverse coherence re- duction factor FI to an elliptical trajectory dictated by sharpness α and bias β. (b) Linear reduction model. An incoherent probabilis- tic mixture (Σ) of completely dephasing (P±) and non-disturbing (I) branches yields a linear scaling FII. The elliptical mode strictly pro- vides greater backaction suppression (FI ≥FII).

FIG. 2. Correlation sharing boundaries in the bilateral sequential sce- nario. The blue dotted curve illustrates the Pareto front (maximum S2 for fixed S1) under generalized measurements, derived via global optimization over 100 discrete samples with specific optimal con- figurations. Gray dashed lines denote the classical bound (S = 2), while the light-gray shaded region highlights simultaneous CHSH violations (S1, S2 > 2). Upper (lower) panels correspond to the el- liptical (linear) reduction models.
